lesson.menu_title: Where Are We? -- Speaking Up in the Status Meeting
lesson.menu_desc: Give your project status out loud: on track, blocked, at risk -- 30 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 8 -- Where Are We? -- Speaking Up in the Status Meeting
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Diogo Leal | Aula 8 -- Where Are We?

paras: Every week, project managers around the world stand up and give a status update. Many of them do it badly. They start at the beginning, list every task, and lose the room in forty seconds.

A good update is short, and it answers three questions in this order. Where are we overall? What is done so far? What is blocked? Leaders do not want the whole story. They want to know if the project is on track, and what you need from them.
The best updates end with one sentence: the next step, with a date. If you say what you will do and when, nobody has to ask.

choices: A project manager should describe every task in detail.
A good update answers three questions and ends with the next step.
Leaders prefer long updates with the full history of the project.

items: A good update starts at the beginning of the project.
It starts with the overall status, not the history.
Leaders want to know if the project is on track.
That, and what you need from them.
A strong update finishes with the next step and a date.
One sentence: what you will do and when.

bank.label: Your four-part update
items: So far, we have finished the design.
We are a little behind on testing.
We have one blocker: the license approval.
The next milestone is the pilot in June.
I need a decision on the license by Friday.

items.situation: Your manager stops you in the corridor: 'How is Atlas going?' You have twenty seconds.
tips: One thing done, one thing blocked.

items.situation: In the status meeting, the project is behind schedule. Say it.
tips: Give the reason in one sentence.

items.situation: A stakeholder asks: 'Is the June milestone at risk?'
tips: Then say what would remove the risk.

items.situation: Leadership asks what you need from them.
Someone asks what the team has done so far this month.
Your update is finished. Close it.
tips: Give the next step and the date.

pressure.title: Answer live
items: Why did you not tell us about the blocker last week?
Can you still deliver in June without the license?
What is your plan B if the approval never arrives?

pressure.text: Full sentences only. Start with the answer, then the reason -- never the other way around.

listenings.text: Hi Diogo, this is Sophia. Tomorrow you give the status update to the leadership team. Please keep it short. Start with the overall status: are we on track, or behind schedule? Then tell us what is done so far, and what is blocked. If a milestone is at risk, say it early, not at the end. And finish with the next step and a date. Two minutes is enough. Thank you, and see you tomorrow.

Good morning. Here is the status of Project Atlas. Overall, we are on track. So far, we have finished the design and the first round of tests. The team has completed two milestones this month. We have not started the migration yet, because we have one blocker: the license approval is still pending. For that reason, the June milestone is at risk. My next step is simple. I need a decision on the license by Friday. If I have it, we keep the June date. That is all from me. Questions are welcome.

extra_audio.text: Sophia asks Diogo for the status of Project Atlas. Diogo says the project is on track overall. He explains that the migration has not started, because of one blocker. Sophia asks what he needs from her. Diogo asks for a decision on the license by Friday.

plan_row.tema: Where Are We? -- Speaking Up in the Status Meeting
plan_row.foco: Present perfect for progress (so far / yet) + past simple
plan_row.homework: Record a 2-min status update of a real project
